Design and development process documentation** | The SOP focuses on validation execution, but it does not require documentation of the complete design/development process, design choices, assumptions, algorithmic logic, architecture, optimization objectives, trade-offs, or personnel/groups involved in development. | **Article 17(1)(b)** requires QMS procedures for design, design control, and design verification; **Article 17(1)(c)** requires development, quality control, and QA procedures; **Article 11(1) / Annex IV** require technical documentation sufficient to demonstrate compliance. |
| **5. Third-party / pre-trained component documentation** | The SOP covers third-party algorithms in scope and vendor risk in §6.2, but it does not require documenting how third-party, licensed, pre-trained, or externally supplied AI components/tools are integrated, modified, validated, and controlled. | **Article 11(1) / Annex IV** requires technical documentation to describe development methods and system components; **Article 17(1)(c)** requires development QA/QC procedures. |
| **6. Full data management lifecycle** | SOP §5.2 covers acquisition, de-identification, hold-out creation, and lockbox controls for validation data, but it does not fully cover training/development data governance, data labelling governance beyond gold-standard validation, data retention, filtration, mining, aggregation, storage, and other pre-market data operations. | **Article 17(1)(f)** expressly requires QMS systems and procedures for data acquisition, collection, analysis, labelling, storage, filtration, mining, aggregation, retention, and any other data operation before placing on the market or putting into service. |
| **7. Risk management system integration** | The SOP references FMEA and SOP-CLIN-002, and escalation in §8, but it does not embed or require a documented Article 9 risk management system, risk file linkage, residual-risk acceptance, risk-control verification, or risk-benefit rationale for each CDSS release. | **Article 17(1)(g)** requires the QMS to include the risk management system referred to in **Article 9**. **Article 11(1)** also expects technical documentation capable of demonstrating compliance, including risk-related evidence. |
| **8. Human oversight documentation** | The SOP says the CDSS is advisory and must not replace clinical judgment (§4.1), but it does not require documented human oversight measures, human-machine interface controls, deployer instructions for oversight, override procedures, or evidence that outputs are interpretable to clinicians. | High-risk AI documentation must support compliance with the Article 14 human-oversight requirement through **Article 11(1) / Annex IV**; Article 17 also requires design/design-control procedures under **Article 17(1)(b)**. |
| **9. Robustness, accuracy, and cybersecurity as regulatory requirements** | The SOP includes clinical performance metrics and some security controls, but it does not require a regulatory test plan covering expected accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity, known foreseeable conditions affecting performance, or acceptance criteria tied to EU AI Act requirements. | **Article 11(1)** requires technical documentation demonstrating compliance. **Article 17(1)(d)** requires examination, test, and validation procedures before, during, and after development, including frequency. |
| **10. Harmonised standards / common specifications strategy** | SOP §10.2 lists standards such as IEC 62304 and ISO 13485, but it does not require documenting which harmonised standards or common specifications are applied, gaps where standards are not fully applied, or alternative means of compliance. | **Article 17(1)(e)** requires technical specifications, including standards, and the means used to ensure compliance where harmonised standards are not applied in full or do not cover all requirements. |
| **11. Conformity assessment process** | The SOP does not define how CDSS products enter the applicable EU AI Act conformity assessment route, who owns it, what evidence package is submitted, or how notified-body findings are resolved. | **Article 17(1)(a)** requires a regulatory compliance strategy including conformity assessment procedures; **Article 16(f)** requires providers to ensure the relevant conformity assessment before placing on the market or putting into service. |
| **12. EU declaration of conformity and CE marking interface** | The SOP does not require creation/approval/maintenance of the EU declaration of conformity or CE-marking workflow for high-risk AI systems. | **Article 16(g)** requires an EU declaration of conformity; **Article 16(h)** requires CE marking; **Article 17(1)(a)** requires regulatory compliance procedures supporting these obligations. |
| **13. Registration obligations** | The SOP does not address EU AI Act registration obligations for high-risk AI systems, including ownership and timing. | **Article 16(i)** requires providers to comply with registration obligations; this should be part of the QMS regulatory compliance strategy under **Article 17(1)(a)**. |
| **14. Modification/change management under the AI Act** | SOP §10.1 references SOP-CLIN-005, and §8 covers exceptions, but SOP-CLIN-001 itself does not define AI Act-specific modification controls, substantial-change triggers, re-validation thresholds, technical-file updates, or conformity reassessment triggers. | **Article 17(1)(a)** specifically requires procedures for management of modifications to the high-risk AI system. **Article 11(1)** requires technical documentation to be kept up to date. |
| **15. Post-market monitoring system under Article 72** | SOP §7 has post-deployment surveillance, silent trials, KPIs, and reports, but it does not explicitly establish the Article 72 post-market monitoring system, including plan ownership, data collection methods, regulatory outputs, and linkage back to risk management and technical documentation. | **Article 17(1)(h)** requires setup, implementation, and maintenance of a post-market monitoring system in accordance with **Article 72**. |
| **16. Serious-incident reporting procedure** | SOP §8.2 says findings are reported to regulatory bodies, but it lacks EU AI Act-specific serious-incident criteria, timelines, report contents, responsibilities, and authority-routing. | **Article 17(1)(i)** requires procedures for reporting serious incidents in accordance with **Article 73**. A generic “reported to regulatory bodies” statement is insufficient. |
| **17. Communications with authorities, notified bodies, operators, customers, and interested parties** | The SOP identifies Regulatory Affairs as a liaison role, but does not establish procedures/templates for communications with national competent authorities, notified bodies, other operators, customers/deployers, or interested parties. | **Article 17(1)(j)** requires QMS procedures for handling communications with national competent authorities, notified bodies, other operators, customers, and other interested parties. |
| **18. Record-keeping system and retention period** | SOP §5.6 stores the signed deployment artifact and §6.1 logs validation activity, but the SOP does not define retention of all relevant QMS and technical documentation, including retention duration, records index, legal hold, and retrieval obligations. | **Article 17(1)(k)** requires systems and procedures for record-keeping of all relevant documentation and information. Related provider obligations also require keeping technical/QMS documentation available to authorities. |
| **19. Resource management and security of supply** | The SOP assigns roles and training, but it does not address resource planning, competence capacity, infrastructure availability, supplier continuity, model/service dependencies, or security-of-supply measures. | **Article 17(1)(l)** requires resource management, including security-of-supply related measures. |
| **20. Management accountability framework for the full QMS** | SOP §3 has a RACI for validation activities, but it does not establish accountability for all Article 17 QMS aspects, such as regulatory strategy, design controls, development QA, standards, risk management, post-market monitoring, serious incidents, authority communication, recordkeeping, resources, and modifications. | **Article 17(1)(m)** requires an accountability framework setting out management and staff responsibilities for all Article 17 QMS aspects. |
| **21. QMS scope is too narrow** | SOP-CLIN-001 governs validation, not the complete provider QMS. It relies on related SOPs, but does not show how they collectively satisfy Article 17 or how conflicts/gaps are controlled. | **Article 17(1)** requires the provider to put in place a documented QMS that ensures compliance with the Regulation and includes all listed aspects. |
| **22. Single technical documentation for MDR-linked products** | The scope includes diagnostic imaging algorithms with CE marking under EU MDR, but the SOP does not require a single integrated technical documentation set where the AI system is related to a product covered by Union harmonisation legislation. | **Article 11(2)** requires a single set of technical documentation for high-risk AI systems related to products covered by Union harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I, Section A, containing the AI Act information and the relevant sectoral-law information. |

## Practical remediation items to add to SOP-CLIN-001

1. Add an **EU AI Act Technical Documentation Procedure** with an Annex IV checklist, artifact owners, approval gates, repositories, versioning, and update triggers.  
2. Add an **Article 17 QMS crosswalk** showing which SOP covers each QMS element, with SOP-CLIN-001 linking to design control, development QA, risk management, PMS, serious incident, change control, authority communications, and record retention.  
3. Add a **Regulatory Compliance and Conformity Assessment section** covering conformity assessment, notified body interaction, EU declaration of conformity, CE marking, and registration.  
4. Add a **Technical File Traceability Matrix** mapping requirements → evidence → owner → approval → storage location → retention period.  
5. Expand data governance to include **training, validation, testing, labelling, cleaning, retention, aggregation, and provenance** across the full AI lifecycle.  
6. Add explicit procedures for **Article 72 post-market monitoring** and **Article 73 serious incident reporting**.  
7. Add management accountability and resource/security-of-supply controls required by **Article 17(1)(l)–(m)**.

Compliance level: partial

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer comprehensively identifies all major missing elements from the SOP against Article 11/Annex IV and Article 17, and correctly cites the relevant EU AI Act articles and annexes throughout.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-LEGC-005 (Regulatory Filing and Reporting) against GDPR's transparency and transfer obligations, what specific elements are MISSING?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or alternative Chapter V transfer mechanisms for cross-border filings — international transfers are acknowledged but the operational mechanism is not specified; (2) transfer-impact-assessment procedures considering the legal environment of recipient jurisdictions; (3) Article 13/14 specificity in privacy notices triggered by filing-related processing — purposes, lawful basis, recipients (regulators), retention, data-subject rights; (4) supplementary measures (encryption, pseudonymisation) tied to high-risk filings; (5) records of processing (Article 30) specifically for filing-related processing activities.

output.answer:
SOP-LEGC-005 has only a high-level GDPR statement: it says data subjects are informed through website privacy notices and that “appropriate safeguards” are used for EU-to-US transfers for regulatory submissions (SOP-LEGC-005 §4.6). Against GDPR transparency and transfer obligations, the following specific elements are missing.

| Area | Missing element | Why it matters / GDPR citation |
|---|---|---|
| Transparency governance | **No filing-specific transparency trigger** requiring the RFO/DPO to confirm whether a regulatory filing uses EU personal data and whether a notice update or direct notice is required. | GDPR requires information under Articles 13 and 14 to be provided to data subjects in a “concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible” form using clear and plain language (GDPR Art. 12(1)). |
| Privacy notice content | **No required content checklist for privacy notices covering regulatory filings.** The SOP does not require the notice to include controller identity/contact details, DPO contact, processing purposes, legal basis, legitimate interests where applicable, recipients/categories of recipients, retention period/criteria, data subject rights, complaint rights, consent withdrawal rights where applicable, statutory/contractual requirement details, or automated decision-making information. | These are required information elements when data is collected from the data subject (GDPR Art. 13(1)–(2)). For data not collected from the data subject, GDPR also requires categories of personal data and source information where applicable (GDPR Art. 14(1)–(2); GDPR Art. 15(1)). |
| Indirect collection | **No procedure for Article 14 notices when regulatory filing data comes from internal systems, customers, partners, vendors, or regulators rather than directly from the data subject.** | Where personal data is not obtained from the data subject, the controller must provide Article 14 information, including categories of personal data, recipients, transfer details, retention, rights, and source information (GDPR Art. 14(1)–(2)). |
| Timing of transparency notices | **No timing rules for when notice must be provided.** The SOP does not require notice at collection, within the Article 14 period, at first communication, at first disclosure, or before further processing for a new purpose. | Article 13 information must be provided at the time personal data is obtained, and before further processing for another purpose (GDPR Art. 13(1), 13(3)). Article 14 information must be provided within a reasonable period, no later than one month, or at first communication/disclosure where applicable, and before further processing for another purpose (GDPR Art. 14(3)–(4)). |
| Further processing for filings | **No compatibility/further-processing notice step for using data originally collected for clinical, payment, HR, or analytics purposes in regulatory filings.** | If the controller intends further processing for a purpose other than the original collection purpose, it must provide information on that other purpose and relevant further information before the further processing (GDPR Art. 13(3); GDPR Art. 14(4)). |
| Data subject rights linkage | **No mechanism to ensure regulatory-filing processing is reflected in access-response content.** The SOP does not require disclosure of purposes, categories, recipients, retention, rights, complaint rights, source, automated decision-making, or transfer safeguards in response to access requests. | GDPR access rights include information on purposes, categories, recipients including third-country recipients, retention, rights, complaint rights, source, automated decision-making, and Article 46 transfer safeguards where personal data is transferred internationally (GDPR Art. 15(1)). |
| Transfer basis decisioning | **No transfer decision tree identifying the lawful Chapter V basis for each regulatory filing transfer.** The SOP only says “appropriate safeguards are in place,” without requiring selection/documentation of adequacy, Article 46 safeguards, BCRs, or Article 49 derogations. | Any transfer to a third country or international organisation must comply with Chapter V so GDPR-level protection is not undermined (GDPR Art. 44). Transfers may rely on an adequacy decision (GDPR Art. 45), appropriate safeguards with enforceable rights and effective remedies (GDPR Art. 46), BCRs (GDPR Art. 47), or limited derogations (GDPR Art. 49). |
| Adequacy documentation | **No requirement to identify whether the recipient country/sector is covered by an EU adequacy decision.** | Transfers may occur where the Commission has decided the relevant third country, territory, sector, or international organisation ensures an adequate level of protection (GDPR Art. 45(1)). |
| Appropriate safeguards detail | **No requirement to specify the safeguard used**, such as SCCs, BCRs, approved codes of conduct, certification mechanisms, or approved contractual/administrative clauses. | Article 46 lists permissible safeguards and requires enforceable data subject rights and effective legal remedies (GDPR Art. 46(1)–(3)). |
| Data subject transfer transparency | **No requirement that privacy notices identify third-country/international-organisation transfers, adequacy status, safeguards, and how to obtain a copy or access where safeguards are made available.** | Articles 13 and 14 require notice of intended transfers to third countries/international organisations, adequacy status, and reference to appropriate/suitable safeguards and how to obtain a copy or access them (GDPR Art. 13(1)(f); GDPR Art. 14(1)(f)). |
| Transfer information on access request | **No process to provide transfer safeguard information when a data subject exercises access rights.** | Where personal data is transferred to a third country or international organisation, the data subject has the right to be informed of the Article 46 appropriate safeguards relating to the transfer (GDPR Art. 15(2)). |
| Article 49 derogation governance | **No controls for exceptional transfer derogations.** The SOP does not require explicit consent with risk disclosure, public-interest/legal-claims/vital-interest assessment, or documentation that a non-repetitive transfer affects only a limited number of data subjects and is based on compelling legitimate interests. | Article 49 permits derogations only in specific situations, including explicit consent after risk disclosure, necessity for important public interest, legal claims, vital interests, or other narrow conditions (GDPR Art. 49(1)). For the residual compelling-legitimate-interest derogation, the transfer must be non-repetitive, limited, necessary, assessed, safeguarded, and the supervisory authority and data subject must be informed (GDPR Art. 49(1), second subparagraph). |
| Supervisory authority notice for residual derogation | **No step to inform the supervisory authority when relying on the residual compelling-legitimate-interest transfer basis.** | Article 49 requires the controller to inform the supervisory authority when using the residual compelling-legitimate-interest basis (GDPR Art. 49(1), second subparagraph). |
| Data subject notice for residual derogation | **No step to inform the data subject of the transfer and compelling legitimate interests when using the residual Article 49 basis.** | Article 49 requires the controller, in addition to Articles 13 and 14 information, to inform the data subject of the transfer and the compelling legitimate interests pursued (GDPR Art. 49(1), second subparagraph). |
| Onward transfers | **No control over onward transfers after EU personal data is sent to US analytics teams or regulators.** | GDPR Chapter V applies to transfers and onward transfers from a third country or international organisation to another third country or international organisation (GDPR Art. 44). |
| Third-country regulator demands | **No procedure for non-EU regulator or administrative authority demands for EU personal data**, such as determining whether an international agreement or MLAT applies. | A third-country court, tribunal, or administrative authority decision requiring transfer/disclosure may be recognised or enforceable only if based on an international agreement such as an MLAT, without prejudice to other Chapter V transfer grounds (GDPR Art. 48). |
| ROPA / transfer records | **No requirement to update ROPA entries for regulatory filing processing and transfers.** SOP-LEGC-005 mentions ROPA summaries only when requested by a supervisory authority, not as an operational control for filings. | Records of processing must include purposes, categories of data subjects/data, recipients including third-country recipients, transfers to third countries or international organisations, identification of the third country/international organisation, and documentation of suitable safeguards for certain Article 49 transfers (GDPR Art. 30(1)). |
| Processor/vendor transfer controls | **No requirement that vendors/processors involved in filing preparation process EU personal data only on documented instructions, including transfer instructions.** | Processors must process personal data only on documented controller instructions, including with regard to transfers to third countries or international organisations, unless legally required otherwise (GDPR Art. 28(3)(a)). |

Bottom line: SOP-LEGC-005 acknowledges GDPR generally, but it does not operationalize GDPR transparency or international-transfer requirements for regulatory filings. The main gaps are lack of notice-content requirements, timing rules, further-processing checks, transfer-basis documentation, safeguard specification, Article 49 derogation governance, onward-transfer controls, and ROPA/processor linkage.

Compliance level: partial

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer comprehensively identifies all missing elements from the reference, including SCCs/transfer mechanisms, transfer impact assessments, Article 13/14 notice specifics, supplementary measures, and Article 30 records, and correctly cites GDPR Articles 13, 14, 30, and Chapter V.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-ITOP-005 (Capacity Planning and Management) against SOC 2 Common Criteria CC7 (monitoring) and CC8 (change management), what specific controls are MISSING?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) scheduled tabletop exercises for incident-response scenarios (CC7.4) — the plan exists but periodic testing is absent; (2) defined alert thresholds and escalation paths for capacity-related anomalies (CC7.2/7.3); (3) explicit segregation of duties between change requester, approver, and implementer for capacity changes (CC8.1); (4) post-incident review procedures feeding lessons learned back to monitoring and change processes (CC7.5).

output.answer:
Below are the **specific missing controls** in SOP-ITOP-005 when assessed against **SOC 2 Common Criteria CC7 (System Operations / monitoring) and CC8.1 (Change Management)**.

## Missing CC7 monitoring / system operations controls

1. **Security-focused configuration monitoring is missing**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC7.1 expects detection and monitoring procedures to identify configuration changes that introduce vulnerabilities, including use of defined configuration standards and monitoring infrastructure/software for noncompliance with those standards.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** The SOP establishes performance baselines and capacity thresholds, but it does not define secure configuration baselines or monitoring for configuration drift/noncompliance. Section 5.1 defines performance baselines; it does not define hardened configuration standards.  
   - **Missing control:** Define hardened configuration standards for in-scope infrastructure and implement continuous monitoring for configuration drift and noncompliance.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC7.1 — “Uses Defined Configuration Standards” and “Monitors Infrastructure and Software.”

2. **Unauthorized change / file integrity monitoring is missing**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC7.1 expects change-detection mechanisms, such as file integrity monitoring, to alert personnel to unauthorized modifications of critical system, configuration, or content files.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** The SOP relies on IaC, GitHub branch protection, and CloudTrail logging for break-glass changes, but it does not require file integrity monitoring or equivalent unauthorized modification detection for critical files/configurations.  
   - **Missing control:** Implement and monitor file/configuration integrity controls for critical infrastructure, application configuration, Terraform state, Helm values, and production configuration artifacts.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC7.1 — “Implements Change-Detection Mechanisms.”

3. **Detection of unknown or unauthorized components is missing**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC7.1 expects procedures to detect introduction of unknown or unauthorized components.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** The SOP lists in-scope assets and references CMDB resources, but it does not define a control to detect unauthorized infrastructure, software, containers, nodes, services, or cloud resources.  
   - **Missing control:** Implement cloud asset inventory reconciliation and unauthorized component detection, such as AWS Config, CSPM, container image inventory, EKS node/workload inventory, and CMDB drift checks.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC7.1 — “Detects Unknown or Unauthorized Components.”

4. **Vulnerability scanning tied to capacity changes is missing**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC7.1 expects infrastructure and software vulnerability scans periodically and after significant changes, with timely remediation of deficiencies.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** Scaling procedures require `terraform plan` or `helm diff`, but do not require vulnerability, misconfiguration, container image, IaC, or cloud posture scans before or after significant capacity changes.  
   - **Missing control:** Require vulnerability and misconfiguration scanning after significant infrastructure scaling or configuration changes, with remediation SLAs and evidence retained in the change record.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC7.1 — “Conducts Vulnerability Scans.”

5. **Security event detection governance is not defined in this SOP**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC7.2 expects detection policies, procedures, and tools to identify potential intrusions, inappropriate access, anomalies, and unusual activity, including governance for security event detection and management.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** Section 5.2 monitors capacity and availability metrics such as CPU, memory, latency, error rate, Kafka lag, and synthetic checks, but it does not define SIEM/security monitoring, suspicious access monitoring, intrusion detection, or unusual activity logging.  
   - **Missing control:** Add explicit integration with security monitoring tools and procedures, including SIEM alerting, access anomaly detection, intrusion detection, and suspicious activity logging for capacity-related systems.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC7.2 — “Implements Detection Policies, Procedures, and Tools.”

6. **Threat intelligence / newly discovered threat intake is missing**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC7.2 includes use of intelligence sources to identify newly discovered threats and vulnerabilities.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** The SOP mentions vulnerability disclosure intake triage as an ISO responsibility, but it does not define how threat intelligence or newly discovered vulnerabilities are monitored and applied to capacity infrastructure.  
   - **Missing control:** Define threat intelligence intake, ownership, review cadence, and triggering criteria for capacity infrastructure remediation or emergency changes.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC7.2 — “Implements Detection Policies, Procedures, and Tools.”

7. **Anomaly triage to determine whether capacity anomalies are security events is incomplete**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC7.2 requires anomalies to be analyzed to determine whether they represent security events, and management should implement procedures to filter, summarize, and analyze anomalies to identify security events.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** Section 5.2 creates incidents for capacity threshold breaches, but the SOP does not require security triage of anomalies such as unexplained traffic spikes, CPU spikes, Kafka lag, sudden network throughput increases, or DDoS-like patterns.  
   - **Missing control:** Require SRE/ISO triage criteria to determine whether capacity anomalies are security events, including escalation to security incident response when indicators suggest malicious activity.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC7.2 — “Implements Filters to Analyze Anomalies.”

8. **Monitoring of detection-tool effectiveness is missing**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC7.2 expects management to monitor and maintain the effectiveness of detection tools.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** The SOP includes Datadog dashboards, monitors, dead-man’s switch checks, and PagerDuty escalation, but does not require periodic testing of alert logic, monitor coverage, alert routing, or detection-tool health.  
   - **Missing control:** Add periodic control testing for Datadog monitors, PagerDuty routes, ServiceNow ticket generation, synthetic checks, and security detection integrations.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC7.2 — “Monitors Detection Tools for Effective Operation.”

9. **Incident response testing / tabletop exercises are explicitly missing**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC7.3 expects procedures for responding to security incidents and periodic evaluation of those procedures; CC7.5 expects incident-recovery plan testing on a periodic basis.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** Section 4.5 states that incident response procedures exist for capacity-driven outages but “regular tabletop exercises to rehearse response procedures are not currently scheduled.”  
   - **Missing control:** Establish periodic tabletop exercises or recovery tests for capacity-related incidents, including DDoS-like capacity exhaustion, cloud-region capacity constraints, database saturation, Kafka backlog, and key-person unavailability scenarios.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC7.3 — “Responds to Security Incidents”; SOC 2 CC7.5 — “Implements Incident-Recovery Plan Testing.”

10. **Incident communication protocols to affected parties are incomplete**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC7.4 requires timely communication of security incidents and actions taken to affected parties; CC7.5 requires communication about the nature of the incident, recovery actions, and prevention activities to management and others as appropriate.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** The SOP requires stakeholder maintenance notifications for some vertical scaling changes and internal reporting, but it does not define customer, regulator, vendor, or external stakeholder communication for capacity-related security incidents or availability-impacting events.  
   - **Missing control:** Define communication triggers, templates, owners, timelines, and approval paths for internal and external notifications related to capacity incidents.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC7.4 — “Develops and Implements Communication of Security Incidents”; SOC 2 CC7.5 — “Communicates Information About the Incident.”

---

## Missing CC8 change management controls

1. **Pre-design authorization is incomplete**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC8.1 requires authorization of system and architecture changes prior to design, development, acquisition, and configuration.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** Section 4.4 requires a ServiceNow Change Request for production capacity changes, but the horizontal scaling runbook in Section 5.4.1 begins with creating a Git branch and PR, and does not require a ServiceNow Change Request before design/configuration work starts.  
   - **Missing control:** Require a Change Request or pre-authorized standard change record before engineers modify Terraform, Helm, or capacity configuration artifacts.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC8.1 — “Authorizes Changes.”

2. **Horizontal scaling procedure does not explicitly create or link a ServiceNow Change Request**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC8.1 expects changes to be tracked and approved prior to implementation.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** Section 4.4 says production capacity changes must be authorized via ServiceNow, but Section 5.4.1 only requires a PR approval and then updating the ServiceNow Incident/Alert record; it does not require creating or linking a ServiceNow Change Request for horizontal scaling.  
   - **Missing control:** Require every production horizontal scaling change to have a linked ServiceNow CHG record, even if categorized as a pre-approved standard change.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC8.1 — “Tracks System Changes” and “Approves System Changes.”

3. **Testing prior to production implementation is insufficient**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC8.1 requires testing internally developed and acquired system changes before production implementation; examples include unit, integration, regression, static/dynamic testing, QA, or automated testing.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** Section 5.4.1 requires `terraform plan` or `helm diff`, but these are impact/diff checks, not full testing. Section 5.4.2 for vertical scaling does not require staging validation, automated tests, failover tests, performance tests, or post-change test evidence before implementation.  
   - **Missing control:** Require documented pre-production testing or risk-based test waivers for capacity changes, including infrastructure validation, performance testing, rollback testing, and dependency checks.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC8.1 — “Tests System Changes.”

4. **Secure design review for capacity changes is missing**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC8.1 expects system changes to be designed and developed in a secure manner to support achievement of objectives.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** The SOP consults the ISO on security implications in the RACI, but the scaling runbooks do not require security review, threat assessment, architecture review, or secure configuration validation for capacity changes.  
   - **Missing control:** Add security review criteria for capacity changes, including network exposure, IAM impact, encryption, logging, hardening, data residency, segmentation, and security group changes.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC8.1 — “Designs and Develops Changes.”

5. **Configuration baseline management for IT technology is missing**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC8.1 expects a baseline configuration of IT and control systems to be created and maintained.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** Section 5.1 creates performance baselines, but the SOP does not create or maintain baseline configurations for infrastructure, Kubernetes, databases, network, monitoring, or CI/CD components.  
   - **Missing control:** Maintain approved baseline configurations for in-scope capacity components and reconcile changes against those baselines.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC8.1 — “Creates Baseline Configuration of IT Technology.”

6. **Rollback planning is not required for all production capacity changes**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC8.1 requires changes to be managed, approved, tested, and implemented through a controlled process that supports entity objectives.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** Section 5.4.2 requires a rollback plan for vertical scaling, but Section 5.4.1 does not require a rollback plan for horizontal scaling, Kafka partition expansion, read-replica changes, storage expansion, or other capacity changes.  
   - **Missing control:** Require rollback/backout plans for all nontrivial production capacity changes, with documented validation criteria and owner.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC8.1 — “Manages Changes Throughout the System Life Cycle” and “Deploys System Changes.”

7. **Emergency change process is incomplete**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC8.1 expects a process for authorizing, designing, testing, approving, and implementing emergency changes.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** Section 8.3 allows the CTO to override standard change management lead times, but the SOP does not define emergency change authorization, minimum testing, approval, retrospective review, evidence, or post-implementation approval requirements.  
   - **Missing control:** Define emergency capacity change workflow, including who can approve, minimum documentation, required post-implementation review, retrospective ServiceNow CHG creation, and after-the-fact approval.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC8.1 — “Provides for Changes Necessary in Emergency Situations.”

8. **Patch-change management is not addressed**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC8.1 expects a process to identify, evaluate, test, approve, and implement patches in a timely manner on infrastructure and software.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** The SOP does not address patching of capacity-related infrastructure such as EKS nodes, AMIs, database engines, Kafka, Redis, Snowflake connectors, monitoring agents, or CI/CD tooling.  
   - **Missing control:** Add reference to the patch management SOP or define patch controls for capacity infrastructure, including evaluation, testing, approval, deployment, and timeliness SLAs.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC8.1 — “Manages Patch Changes.”

9. **Changes required to remediate incidents are not consistently fed into change management**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC8.1 expects infrastructure, data, software, and procedure changes required to remediate incidents to be identified and the change process initiated when identified.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** Section 5.5 requires PIR action items in Jira, but it does not require those remediation items to be converted into ServiceNow change records when they involve infrastructure, software, data, or procedure changes.  
   - **Missing control:** Require PIR remediation actions that change systems or procedures to be linked to formal change records and tracked through closure.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC8.1 — “Identifies Changes in Infrastructure, Data, Software, and Procedures Required to Remediate Incidents.”

10. **Procedure-change management is weak**
   - **SOC 2 requirement:** CC8.1 applies to changes to infrastructure, data, software, and procedures.  
   - **Gap in SOP-ITOP-005:** The SOP has a revision history, but it does not define how changes to the SOP itself, runbooks, threshold formulas, escalation procedures, or monitoring procedures are requested, risk-assessed, tested, approved, and implemented.  
   - **Missing control:** Define formal change control for capacity procedures and runbooks, including approval authority, version control, impact assessment, communication, and training updates.  
   - **Citation:** SOC 2 CC8.1 — criterion text covering changes to “infrastructure, data, software, and procedures.”

## Bottom line

SOP-ITOP-005 is strong for **capacity monitoring, alerting, IaC-based implementation, PR approvals, segregation of duties, and post-incident reviews**, but it is missing several SOC 2 CC7/CC8 controls around **security monitoring, vulnerability/configuration monitoring, unauthorized component detection, incident-response testing, complete ServiceNow change tracking, pre-production testing, emergency change governance, patch changes, and procedure-change control**.

Compliance level: partial

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer identifies many relevant gaps but misses the reference's emphasis on segregation of duties (CC8.1) and post-incident review feedback (CC7.5), and while it cites CC7.1-CC7.5 and CC8.1 broadly, it does not precisely map to the expected CC7.2-CC7.5 and CC8.1 sections for the four key missing controls.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-LEGC-005 (Regulatory Filing and Reporting) against HIPAA's breach-reporting and de-identification requirements, what specific elements are MISSING?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) de-identification methodology for PHI appearing in filings — neither Safe Harbor (§164.514(b)(2)) nor Expert Determination (§164.514(b)(1)) is specified; (2) breach-notification procedures with timelines (§164.404 60-day individual notice, §164.408 HHS reporting), required notice content, and the low-probability-of-compromise risk assessment; (3) minimum-necessary policy (§164.502(b)) for PHI accessible to filing personnel; (4) accounting-of-disclosures procedures (§164.528) for filing-related disclosures.
question.notes: Gap detection paired with the sop_compliance question for the same SOP.

output.answer:
Below are the **specific HIPAA breach-reporting and de-identification elements missing from SOP-LEGC-005**. The SOP has a general regulatory filing workflow, but it does not operationalize HIPAA’s specific breach-notification and de-identification rules.

## A. Missing HIPAA breach-reporting elements

| Missing element | HIPAA requirement | SOP gap |
|---|---|---|
| **HIPAA breach trigger and “unsecured PHI” standard** | HIPAA breach notification applies to a breach of **unsecured protected health information**; “unsecured PHI” means PHI not rendered unusable, unreadable, or indecipherable using HHS-specified technology/methodology. A use/disclosure not permitted by HIPAA is presumed to be a breach unless a low-probability-of-compromise assessment supports otherwise. **45 CFR §164.402** | SOP-LEGC-005 lists HIPAA in scope but does not define the HIPAA breach trigger, “unsecured PHI,” or when a security incident becomes a HIPAA-reportable breach. |
| **Four-factor breach risk assessment** | To rebut the breach presumption, HIPAA requires assessing at least: PHI nature/extent including identifiers and re-identification likelihood; unauthorized recipient; whether PHI was actually acquired/viewed; and mitigation. **45 CFR §164.402(2)(i)–(iv)** | No HIPAA-specific risk assessment workflow, template, required factors, or approval criteria is included. |
| **Discovery-date rule** | A breach is discovered on the first day it is known, or would have been known with reasonable diligence, to the covered entity; knowledge of workforce members or agents counts, except the person committing the breach. **45 CFR §164.404(a)(2)** | SOP does not define HIPAA “discovery,” reasonable diligence, or agent/workforce knowledge rules for starting the notification clock. |
| **Individual notification obligation** | A covered entity must notify each individual whose unsecured PHI was, or is reasonably believed to have been, accessed, acquired, used, or disclosed due to the breach. **45 CFR §164.404(a)(1)** | SOP lacks a HIPAA individual-notification step and does not specify how affected individuals are identified. |
| **HIPAA notification deadline** | Individual notices must be provided without unreasonable delay and no later than **60 calendar days after discovery**, except for law-enforcement delay. **45 CFR §164.404(b); §164.412** | SOP has a general “zero tolerance for late filing,” but no HIPAA-specific 60-calendar-day breach deadline or workflow tied to discovery date. |
| **Required content of individual notices** | Notices must include: what happened, breach and discovery dates if known; types of unsecured PHI involved; protective steps individuals should take; what the entity is doing to investigate, mitigate, and prevent further breaches; and contact procedures including toll-free number, email, website, or postal address. Notices must be in plain language. **45 CFR §164.404(c)(1)–(2)** | SOP does not include a HIPAA breach notice template or required content fields. |
| **Permitted methods of individual notification** | Notice must be by first-class mail to last known address, or email if the individual agreed and has not withdrawn agreement; deceased individuals require notice to next of kin or personal representative where known. **45 CFR §164.404(d)(1)** | SOP does not specify HIPAA-compliant delivery methods for individual notices, email-consent handling, or deceased-individual procedures. |
| **Substitute notice procedures** | If contact information is insufficient/outdated: for fewer than 10 individuals, alternative written notice, telephone, or other means may be used; for 10 or more, conspicuous website posting for 90 days or major media notice, plus a toll-free number active for at least 90 days. **45 CFR §164.404(d)(2)** | SOP lacks substitute-notice triggers, web/media posting requirements, and the 90-day toll-free number requirement. |
| **Urgent additional notice** | If imminent misuse of unsecured PHI is possible, the entity may provide urgent notice by telephone or other means in addition to written notice. **45 CFR §164.404(d)(3)** | SOP does not define urgent HIPAA breach-notification escalation or supplemental telephone/rapid notice procedures. |
| **Notice to HHS Secretary** | For breaches involving **500 or more individuals**, notice to HHS must be contemporaneous with individual notice and submitted as specified on the HHS website. For breaches involving **fewer than 500 individuals**, the entity must maintain a log and submit to HHS no later than 60 days after the end of the calendar year. **45 CFR §164.408(a)–(c)** | SOP does not distinguish ≥500 vs. <500 individual breaches, does not require the annual small-breach log, and does not specify HHS submission timing or method. |
| **Media notification** | For breaches involving more than 500 residents of a state or jurisdiction, the covered entity must notify prominent media outlets serving that state or jurisdiction without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 calendar days after discovery; the content must meet §164.404(c). **45 CFR §164.406(a)–(c)** | SOP does not include the “>500 residents of a state or jurisdiction” media-notice threshold or prominent-media notification procedure. |
| **Business associate breach notification to covered entity** | A business associate must notify the covered entity without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 calendar days after discovery; notice must identify each affected individual to the extent possible and provide other information needed for individual notice. **45 CFR §164.410(a)–(c)** | SOP defines “BA,” but does not state whether Meridian may act as a business associate, nor does it specify BA-to-covered-entity breach notice timing, content, or affected-individual identification. |
| **Business associate discovery rule** | A BA breach is discovered when known, or reasonably should have been known, to the BA; knowledge of employees, officers, or agents counts, except the person committing the breach. **45 CFR §164.410(a)(2)** | SOP does not include BA discovery rules or escalation requirements triggered by BA workforce/agent knowledge. |
| **Law-enforcement delay procedure** | If law enforcement states notice would impede an investigation or harm national security, written delay follows the specified period; oral statements must be documented with the official’s identity and delay may last no more than 30 days unless written confirmation is received. **45 CFR §164.412(a)–(b)** | SOP has general regulator-inquiry handling but no HIPAA law-enforcement delay procedure, documentation requirement, or 30-day oral-delay limit. |
| **Burden-of-proof documentation** | The covered entity or BA bears the burden of proving that required notifications were made or that the incident was not a breach. **45 CFR §164.414(b)** | SOP’s archival controls are general; it does not require a HIPAA breach file containing risk assessment, notification evidence, HHS/media submissions, affected-individual lists, or “not a breach” rationale sufficient to meet the HIPAA burden of proof. |

## B. Missing HIPAA de-identification elements

| Missing element | HIPAA requirement | SOP gap |
|---|---|---|
| **HIPAA de-identification standard** | Health information is de-identified only if it does not identify an individual and there is no reasonable basis to believe it can be used to identify an individual. **45 CFR §164.514(a)** | SOP does not define the HIPAA de-identification standard or require a de-identification determination before using de-identified data in filings. |
| **Choice of permitted de-identification method** | HIPAA allows de-identification only through either **Expert Determination** or **Safe Harbor**. **45 CFR §164.514(b)(1)–(2)** | SOP does not require the filer to select, document, or evidence either HIPAA de-identification method. |
| **Expert Determination qualifications** | Expert Determination must be performed by a person with appropriate knowledge of and experience with generally accepted statistical and scientific principles and methods for rendering information not individually identifiable. **45 CFR §164.514(b)(1)** | SOP does not define who is qualified to perform expert determination, when expert determination is required, or how independence/competence is verified. |
| **Expert Determination risk conclusion** | The expert must determine that the risk is very small that the information could be used, alone or with reasonably available information, by an anticipated recipient to identify an individual. **45 CFR §164.514(b)(1)(i)** | SOP does not require a “very small risk” determination, recipient-specific re-identification analysis, or consideration of reasonably available external data. |
| **Expert Determination documentation** | The expert must document the methods and results justifying the de-identification determination. **45 CFR §164.514(b)(1)(ii)** | SOP does not require methods/results documentation in the Evidence Package for de-identified datasets. |
| **Safe Harbor identifier removal list** | Safe Harbor requires removing specified identifiers of the individual and relatives, employers, or household members, including names; geographic subdivisions smaller than a state subject to ZIP-code rules; date elements except year and ages over 89; phone/fax/email; SSNs; medical record numbers; health plan beneficiary numbers; account numbers; certificate/license numbers; vehicle/device identifiers; URLs; IP addresses; biometric identifiers; full-face images; and other unique identifying numbers, characteristics, or codes. **45 CFR §164.514(b)(2)(i)(A)–(R)** | SOP does not include the 18-identifier Safe Harbor checklist or require verification that each identifier category has been removed. |
| **Safe Harbor ZIP-code and small-population rule** | Initial three digits of ZIP codes may be retained only where the Census-defined geographic unit has more than 20,000 people; otherwise the ZIP code must be changed to 000. **45 CFR §164.514(b)(2)(i)(B)** | SOP does not include ZIP-code population threshold checks or masking rules. |
| **Safe Harbor date and age handling** | All date elements except year directly related to an individual must be removed, and ages over 89 and associated date elements must be aggregated into “90 or older.” **45 CFR §164.514(b)(2)(i)(C)** | SOP does not specify date truncation, age-over-89 aggregation, or validation of date fields in filing datasets. |
| **Actual-knowledge test** | Even after Safe Harbor removal, the covered entity must not have actual knowledge that the information could be used alone or with other information to identify an individual. **45 CFR §164.514(b)(2)(ii)** | SOP does not require an actual-knowledge certification or re-identification-risk attestation before treating data as de-identified. |
| **Re-identification code controls** | A covered entity may assign a re-identification code only if the code is not derived from or related to information about the individual, is not otherwise translatable to identify the individual, is not used/disclosed for another purpose, and the re-identification mechanism is not disclosed. **45 CFR §164.514(c)(1)–(2)** | SOP does not address re-identification keys, tokenization codes, code derivation limits, key custody, or prohibition on disclosing the re-identification mechanism. |
| **Treatment of disclosed re-identification codes as PHI** | Disclosure of a code or other means of record identification designed to enable re-identification constitutes disclosure of PHI; re-identified information may be used or disclosed only as permitted by HIPAA. **45 CFR §164.502(d)(2)(i)–(ii)** | SOP does not warn that sharing re-identification keys/codes in regulatory evidence packages can be a PHI disclosure, nor does it restrict re-identified data handling. |
| **Use/disclosure of PHI to create de-identified information** | A covered entity may use PHI to create de-identified information or disclose PHI to a business associate for that purpose. **45 CFR §164.502(d)(1)** | SOP does not specify controls for using PHI to create de-identified filing data, including BA involvement in de-identification workflows. |

## Bottom line

SOP-LEGC-005 provides a general regulatory filing governance structure, but it is missing the HIPAA-specific operational requirements needed for breach reporting and de-identification. The most significant gaps are: **no HIPAA breach risk-assessment process, no individual/HHS/media notice thresholds and templates, no business-associate breach workflow, no law-enforcement delay procedure, and no de-identification method/checklist/evidence requirements.**

Compliance level: partial

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer comprehensively identifies all missing elements from the reference (de-identification methodology, breach notification procedures/timelines/content, minimum necessary, accounting of disclosures) and correctly cites all expected HIPAA sections plus additional relevant ones.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-AIML-006 (Model Performance Monitoring) against SR 11-7's ongoing monitoring expectations, what specific elements are MISSING?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) defined performance thresholds and back-testing cadence tied to model risk tier — monitoring is referenced but specific KPIs and review frequencies are not pinned; (2) challenger-model requirement for high-impact decisions — SR 11-7 expects benchmark/challenger comparisons; (3) escalation path from monitoring findings to senior management and the board (Section V governance); (4) overarching model-risk framework + model inventory anchoring the monitoring activities; (5) explicit linkage between drift signals and re-validation triggers.

expected_citations.section: Section III (Validation), Section IV (Ongoing Monitoring), Section V (Governance)

question.notes: Gap detection paired with the sop_compliance question for same SOP.

output.answer:
SOP-AIML-006 has a strong technical monitoring design, but it is **missing several SR 11-7 ongoing monitoring elements** that go beyond drift dashboards and incident response.

| Missing element | Why it is required under SR 11-7 | Evidence / gap in SOP-AIML-006 |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Risk/materiality-based monitoring frequency and scope** | SR 11-7 states that the “frequency and scope” of ongoing monitoring reports should depend on the model’s nature, data availability, availability of new modeling approaches, and **model materiality**. *(SR 11-7, V. Model Validation and Monitoring — Ongoing Model Monitoring)* | SOP-AIML-006 sets standard windows and fixed reporting cadences — weekly, monthly, quarterly — but does not assign monitoring intensity by model materiality, risk tier, complexity, or business impact. *(SOP §§5.2, 7.2)* |
| **2. Monitoring for changes in products, exposures, activities, clients, and market/business conditions** | SR 11-7 expects ongoing monitoring to evaluate whether a model performs as expected given changes in “products, exposures, activities, clients, data relevance, or market conditions.” *(SR 11-7, V. Model Validation and Monitoring — Ongoing Model Monitoring)* | The SOP monitors data drift, prediction drift, performance metrics, latency, and feature missingness, but it does not require structured monitoring of business/product changes, client/population shifts, exposure mix, portfolio changes, or market-condition changes. *(SOP §§5.2–5.4)* |
| **3. Periodic reassessment of model limitations over time** | SR 11-7 says an effective ongoing monitoring plan may include regularly assessing model limitations identified at development and over time. *(SR 11-7, V. Model Validation and Monitoring — Ongoing Model Monitoring)* | The SOP does not require a model limitation inventory, periodic limitation review, limitation owner, limitation severity rating, or documented reassessment of whether limitations remain acceptable after deployment. *(SOP §§5, 7, 8)* |
| **4. Continued relevance / intended-use review** | SR 11-7 states that users benefit from periodically reviewing model relevance and understanding and communicating model limitations. *(SR 11-7, V. Model Validation and Monitoring — Model Use)* | The SOP captures business purpose at onboarding, but it does not require periodic review that the model remains relevant to its approved use case, population, decision context, or operating environment. *(SOP §5.1)* |
| **5. Controls for expanded or changed model use** | SR 11-7 states that using a model beyond its intended purpose introduces additional uncertainty and risk and should involve additional analysis, limitation review, and controls. *(SR 11-7, V. Model Validation and Monitoring — Model Use)* | The SOP does not define triggers or approval steps for when a model is used in a new product, population, geography, workflow, or decision context. *(SOP §§1.2, 5.1, 8)* |
| **6. Formal outcomes-analysis review process tied to business objectives** | SR 11-7 defines outcomes analysis as comparing model outputs to real-world outcomes to assess performance relative to model objectives and business use. *(SR 11-7, V. Model Validation and Monitoring — Outcomes Analysis)* | The SOP calculates technical metrics when ground truth is available, but it does not require a documented review of whether outputs remain reasonable and appropriate for the model’s business or clinical objective. *(SOP §5.2)* |
| **7. Back-testing and outlier analysis as routine monitoring activities** | SR 11-7 identifies back-testing and outlier analysis as forms of outcomes analysis that may be performed as part of ongoing monitoring. *(SR 11-7, V. Model Validation and Monitoring — Outcomes Analysis)* | The SOP does not require scheduled back-testing, benchmark-period testing, vintage analysis, adverse-segment outlier review, tail-event review, or exception/outlier sampling outside incident RCA. *(SOP §§5.2, 5.5, 6.2)* |
| **8. Criteria for additional analysis/testing when monitoring results are questionable** | SR 11-7 says outcomes analysis should be reviewed for reasonableness and appropriateness, with additional analysis and testing if warranted. *(SR 11-7, V. Model Validation and Monitoring — Outcomes Analysis)* | The SOP triggers alerts and incident response, but it does not define when monitoring results must be escalated into additional validation testing, independent review, expanded sample review, or challenger analysis. *(SOP §§5.4–5.5)* |
| **9. Formal decision framework for overlays, adjustment, recalibration, or redevelopment** | SR 11-7 states that a model that no longer performs as expected may warrant overlays, adjustment, or redevelopment depending on the organization’s model deterioration policy. *(SR 11-7, V. Model Validation and Monitoring — Ongoing Model Monitoring)* | The SOP includes rollback, traffic shunting, feature-off, redesign, heuristic replacement, and sunsetting, but it lacks explicit decision criteria and approvals for overlays, recalibration, model adjustment, or redevelopment. *(SOP §§5.5, 8.2)* |
| **10. Independent model risk / validation review of monitoring results** | SR 11-7 treats monitoring as part of model validation and emphasizes meaningful critique and sound judgment about model performance and risk. *(SR 11-7, V. Model Validation and Monitoring)* SR 11-7 also expects governance to address clear roles, accountability, and conflicts of interest. *(SR 11-7, VI. Governance and Controls — Roles and Responsibilities)* | The SOP assigns monitoring mainly to MLOps, Model Owners, Engineering, and executives, but it does not define an independent model validation, model risk management, or second-line review role for monitoring results. *(SOP §§5, 7, 8)* |
| **11. Internal audit role for evaluating effectiveness of monitoring controls** | SR 11-7 says internal audit, where part of MRM practices, generally evaluates whether model risk management practices are rigorous, effective, and implemented according to policy. *(SR 11-7, VI. Governance and Controls — Roles and Responsibilities)* | The SOP does not assign Internal Audit any role in assessing whether monitoring controls, alert thresholds, exception handling, or remediation processes are effective. *(SOP §§7–8)* |
| **12. Tracking of monitoring recommendations, responses, and remediation to closure** | SR 11-7 says documentation supports continuity of operations and tracking of recommendations, responses, exceptions, and remediation efforts. *(SR 11-7, VI. Governance and Controls — Documentation)* | The SOP documents incidents, post-mortems, tickets, and exceptions, but it does not require a centralized issue log that tracks monitoring findings, validation recommendations, management responses, remediation owners, due dates, and closure evidence. *(SOP §§5.5, 7, 8.1)* |
| **13. Model inventory fields sufficient to support individual and aggregate model risk management** | SR 11-7 says an effective model inventory should contain sufficient information to understand model risks at both individual and aggregate levels. *(SR 11-7, VI. Governance and Controls — Model Inventory)* | The SOP registry fields include model ID, version, purpose, owner, training dataset snapshot, framework, and dependencies, but they do not include materiality tier, model risk rating, approved use, limitations, validation status, monitoring tier, issue status, or aggregate risk attributes. *(SOP §5.1)* |
| **14. Vendor / third-party model monitoring oversight details** | SR 11-7 states that vendor and third-party models can present unique validation and MRM challenges and should be subject to proper oversight and integration into broader model risk management. *(SR 11-7, VII. Vendor and Other Third-Party Products)* | The SOP includes third-party/procured models in scope, but it does not specify vendor monitoring obligations, required data access, metric transparency, contractual evidence, proprietary-model limitations, or escalation paths with vendors. *(SOP §1.2)* |
| **15. User feedback and complementary analysis process** | SR 11-7 states that user feedback provides business insights that enhance future development and that users benefit from supplementing model output with complementary analysis and information. *(SR 11-7, V. Model Validation and Monitoring — Model Use)* | The SOP does not define a process for model users, clinicians, financial analysts, or operations teams to submit performance concerns, override patterns, qualitative feedback, or complementary evidence into the monitoring process. *(SOP §§5–7)* |

**Bottom line:** SOP-AIML-006 is technically mature for automated performance, drift, alerting, incident response, and reporting. The main SR 11-7 gaps are in **model risk governance, materiality-based monitoring, outcomes-analysis review, limitation reassessment, business-condition monitoring, independent challenge, and documentation of recommendations/remediation**.

Compliance level: partial

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer comprehensively identifies missing SR 11-7 elements with detailed rationale, though it cites 'Section V' for monitoring and governance where the reference expects Sections III, IV, and V, making citations partially misaligned.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-CLIN-005 (Diagnostic AI Accuracy and Calibration) against NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function (including the GenAI Profile where relevant), what specific elements are MISSING?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) bias/fairness/equity testing methodology beyond accuracy — demographic-stratified performance metrics, calibration across protected groups (MEASURE 2/4); (2) AI risk-management framework anchoring measurement to risk tolerance (GOVERN); (3) AI system inventory linking the diagnostic AI to organisational risk (MAP 1); (4) MEASURE coverage of the seven trustworthiness characteristics — safety, security, accountability/transparency, explainability/interpretability, privacy, fair/bias — not just accuracy; (5) post-deployment monitoring closing the loop into MANAGE.
question.notes: Gap detection — clinical AI broader than accuracy.

output.answer:
Below are the **specific missing elements** in SOP-CLIN-005 when assessed against the **NIST AI RMF 1.0 MEASURE function** and, where applicable, the **NIST AI 600-1 Generative AI Profile**.

| Area | Missing element | Basis |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Traceability from mapped risks to measurement methods** | The SOP does not require a **MEASURE traceability matrix** linking each risk identified during MAP—clinical safety, privacy, security, explainability, transparency, misuse, human-AI interaction, environmental impact, etc.—to a specific qualitative or quantitative metric. It focuses mainly on accuracy, calibration, drift, subgroup performance, and operational KPIs. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 1.1**, calls for measurement approaches and metrics for risks identified during MAP, and documentation of risks or trustworthiness characteristics that will not or cannot be measured. SOP-CLIN-005 §§5.1, 5.3, 5.4, 5.8, 7 focus on clinical performance and monitoring but do not require this mapping. |
| **2. Documentation of unmeasured risks** | The SOP does not require teams to document **which trustworthiness characteristics are not measured** and why—for example, privacy leakage, environmental impact, transparency/accountability, misuse risk, or certain safety risks. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 1.1**, states that risks or trustworthiness characteristics not measured should be properly documented. SOP-CLIN-005 has no equivalent requirement. |
| **3. Periodic review of whether selected metrics remain appropriate** | The SOP monitors model performance, calibration, drift, and clinical outcomes, but it does not require periodic review of whether metrics such as ECE, AUC-ROC, sensitivity/specificity, drift scores, or subgroup thresholds remain appropriate for the evolving clinical context. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 1.2**, calls for regular assessment and updating of metric appropriateness and control effectiveness, including reports of errors and impacts on affected communities. SOP-CLIN-005 §7 trends metrics but does not reassess the metric set itself. |
| **4. Formal assessment of control effectiveness** | Controls such as low-confidence suppression, input validation, adversarial filters, audit logs, and drift monitoring are specified, but the SOP does not require recurring testing to determine whether those controls are actually effective at reducing mapped risks. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 1.2**, requires regular assessment of existing control effectiveness. SOP-CLIN-005 §6 lists controls, but does not define control effectiveness tests, acceptance criteria, or review cadence. |
| **5. Engagement of affected communities and broader external stakeholders** | The SOP includes internal clinical validation and an external Medical Advisory Board, but it does not establish structured consultation with **patients, affected communities, end users, client-site operators, or other AI actors external to the development/deployment team** for measurement design or updates. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 1.3**, calls for internal experts or independent assessors and consultation with domain experts, users, external AI actors, and affected communities as needed. SOP-CLIN-005 §§3, 5.4, 5.8 rely mainly on internal roles and clinical advisors. |
| **6. Human-subject protection requirements for evaluations** | The SOP does not explicitly require IRB/ethics review, informed consent analysis, waiver documentation, human-subject protection determinations, or patient-data use approvals for clinical validation studies or human factors testing. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 2.2**, states that evaluations involving human subjects should meet applicable human-subject protection requirements and be representative of the relevant population. SOP-CLIN-005 §§5.4 and 5.5 describe validation and simulated clinical evaluation but do not address human-subject protection controls. |
| **7. Representativeness criteria for evaluation datasets** | The SOP requires an independent validation dataset “ideally” from a different site and requires subgroup analysis, but it does not define explicit representativeness criteria for the intended population, clinical settings, disease prevalence, device types, geography, workflow variation, or site mix. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 2.2**, requires human subjects or datasets to be representative of the relevant population. SOP-CLIN-005 §§5.2, 5.4, 5.8 partially address this, but do not require a representativeness justification against the intended-use population. |
| **8. Documentation of test tools in TEVV with validation of tool suitability** | The SOP names the Meridian Clinical ML Observability Platform and a customized MLflow/LangSmith stack, but it does not require documentation of the tool configuration, versioning, validation status, known limitations, or suitability of those tools for TEVV. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 2.1**, calls for documentation of test sets, metrics, and details about tools used during TEVV. SOP-CLIN-005 §5.3 identifies tools but does not require tool qualification or detailed tool documentation. |
| **9. Generalizability limitations beyond validation conditions** | The SOP does not require a formal **limitations-of-generalizability statement** describing where the model should not be used, such as unsupported populations, sites, imaging devices, EHR configurations, rare disease presentations, workflow differences, or out-of-distribution clinical settings. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 2.5**, calls for documenting limitations of generalizability beyond the conditions under which the technology was developed. SOP-CLIN-005 §§5.4, 5.8 include performance comparison and subgroup mitigation but not a general limitations statement. |
| **10. Production monitoring of full AI-system component behavior** | The SOP monitors latency, error rate, low-confidence rate, drift, and clinical performance, but it does not explicitly monitor the functionality and behavior of all AI-system components—preprocessing, feature pipelines, model wrappers, post-processing, UI rendering, integration APIs, audit logging, and clinical workflow components. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 2.4**, addresses monitoring functionality and behavior of the AI system and components in production. SOP-CLIN-005 §7.1 focuses on service KPIs and drift, not full component-level behavioral monitoring. |
| **11. Regular safety-risk evaluation tied to mapped safety risks** | The SOP addresses clinical performance, calibration, low-confidence handling, adverse event summaries, and incident escalation, but it does not require a recurring safety-risk evaluation framework such as safety case review, hazard analysis, failure mode review, near-miss analysis, or confirmation that the system fails safely outside its knowledge limits. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 2.6**, calls for regular evaluation of safety risks identified in MAP and demonstration that the system can fail safely and gracefully. SOP-CLIN-005 §§6.1, 7, 10 include some safeguards but not a structured recurring safety-risk evaluation. |
| **12. Security and resilience evaluation** | The SOP includes an adversarial input filter, access control, and audit logs, but it does not require regular security and resilience testing such as adversarial robustness evaluation, penetration testing of inference APIs, model extraction/inversion testing, data poisoning assessment, dependency risk testing, or recovery/resilience exercises. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 2.7**, calls for AI system security and resilience to be evaluated and documented. SOP-CLIN-005 §§6.1–6.2 list controls but do not specify recurring evaluation methods or documentation. |
| **13. Transparency and accountability risk measurement** | The SOP requires clinician-facing confidence scores and audit logs, but it does not define metrics or tests for transparency and accountability risks—for example, whether clinicians understand limitations, whether accountability paths are clear, whether audit logs support incident reconstruction, or whether users can identify when AI influenced a decision. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 2.8**, calls for risks associated with transparency and accountability to be examined and documented. SOP-CLIN-005 §§4.5, 5.5, 6.1 include transparency-related controls but not measurement of transparency/accountability risks. |
| **14. Explainability validation** | The SOP requires interpretable presentation of confidence scores, but it does not require formal validation of explanations, explanation fidelity, explanation stability, clinician comprehension of explanations, or documentation of model-level and output-level explainability. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 2.9**, calls for the AI model to be explained, validated, and documented, and for outputs to be interpreted within context. SOP-CLIN-005 §§4.5, 5.5 focus on UI presentation and workflow, not explanation validation. |
| **15. Privacy-risk measurement** | The SOP references HIPAA-compliant logs and hashed patient IDs, but it does not require privacy risk measurement such as re-identification risk, membership inference risk, model inversion risk, leakage in outputs, privacy impact assessment, or privacy testing of logged inference data. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 2.10**, calls for privacy risk of the AI system to be examined and documented. SOP-CLIN-005 §6.1 TC-05 addresses audit logging but not privacy-risk evaluation. |
| **16. Environmental impact and sustainability assessment** | The SOP has no requirement to measure or document energy use, compute intensity, water use, carbon impact, or sustainability tradeoffs for model training, fine-tuning, deployment, or monitoring. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 2.12**, calls for environmental impact and sustainability of AI model training and management activities to be assessed and documented. SOP-CLIN-005 contains no environmental measurement section. |
| **17. Evaluation of TEVV effectiveness** | The SOP requires validation reports and statistical confidence intervals, but it does not require meta-evaluation of the TEVV process itself, such as whether metrics have construct validity, whether sampling methods introduce bias, whether ground-truth adjudication is reliable, or whether chosen metrics meaningfully capture clinical risk. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 2.13**, calls for the effectiveness of TEVV metrics and processes to be evaluated and documented. SOP-CLIN-005 §§5.3–5.4 specify TEVV outputs but not TEVV effectiveness evaluation. |
| **18. Measurement error models and uncertainty for human feedback or adjudication** | The SOP uses confidence intervals for model metrics, but it does not require measurement error models for human chart review, adjudication bias, inter-rater disagreement, clinician concordance data, or structured human feedback. | NIST AI 600-1 Generative AI Profile, **MS-2.13-001**, recommends creating measurement error models for pre-deployment metrics and documenting bias or statistical variance in applied metrics or structured human feedback. SOP-CLIN-005 §§5.4, 7.2 use chart review and clinician agree/disagree fields but do not model those sources of measurement error. |
| **19. Risk tracking for unanticipated and emergent risks** | The SOP tracks known metrics such as drift, calibration, performance, and adverse events, but it does not establish a risk-tracking process for **unanticipated or emergent AI risks** that may not be captured by current metrics. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 3.1**, calls for approaches, personnel, and documentation to regularly identify and track existing, unanticipated, and emergent AI risks based on intended and actual performance in deployed contexts. SOP-CLIN-005 §7 focuses on predefined KPIs and known clinical monitoring outputs. |
| **20. Approach for risks that are difficult to measure or lack mature metrics** | The SOP does not define proxy indicators, qualitative review, expert elicitation, scenario analysis, or other tracking approaches for risks that are hard to quantify, such as automation bias, clinician overreliance, patient trust impacts, health equity harms, or downstream workflow harms. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 3.2**, calls for risk-tracking approaches where AI risks are difficult to assess using available measurement techniques or where metrics are not yet available. SOP-CLIN-005 does not include such a process. |
| **21. End-user and impacted-community reporting and appeal processes** | The SOP includes clinician concordance fields and adverse event summaries, but it does not establish a process for end users, patients, or impacted communities to report problems, challenge outcomes, or appeal AI-related outcomes, nor does it integrate those reports into evaluation metrics. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 3.3**, calls for feedback processes for end users and impacted communities to report problems and appeal system outcomes, integrated into AI system evaluation metrics. SOP-CLIN-005 §§5.5, 7.2, 7.3 do not provide patient/community reporting or appeal mechanisms. |
| **22. Feedback loop on whether measurement approaches work in deployment** | The SOP has monthly and quarterly reviews, but it does not require evaluation of whether measurement approaches themselves are effective in the real deployment context, informed by domain experts and end users. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 4.1**, calls for measurement approaches to be connected to deployment contexts and informed through consultation with domain experts and other end users, with approaches documented. SOP-CLIN-005 §7 reviews outputs but does not review measurement approach efficacy. |
| **23. Deployment-lifecycle validation using input from relevant AI actors** | The SOP’s QBR is internal and executive-focused. It does not require measurement results to be validated with relevant AI actors such as clinicians at client sites, MLOps operators, implementation teams, affected users, external assessors, or patient representatives. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 4.2**, calls for measurement results across deployment context and lifecycle to be informed by domain experts and relevant AI actors to validate whether the system performs as intended. SOP-CLIN-005 §7.3 does not require this broader validation. |
| **24. Documentation of performance improvements or declines based on stakeholder consultation and field data** | The SOP trends performance versus baseline, but it does not require documenting measurable improvements or declines that arise from stakeholder consultation, affected-community input, or field data about context-relevant risks and trustworthiness characteristics. | NIST AI RMF 1.0, **MEASURE 4.3**, calls for measurable performance improvements or declines based on consultations with relevant AI actors, including affected communities, and field data to be identified and documented. SOP-CLIN-005 §7.3 includes trend summaries but not this stakeholder-informed documentation. |
| **25.
